ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean clam kalguksu; closest defensible parent class is 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_03315595).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Korean kalguksu with clams. A0FJP (noodle soup) used as the nearest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Korean noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 700 g, comprising a bowl of clam noodle soup with broth, noodles, and clams, accompanied by small portions of kimchi and pickled radish side dishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — clams mixed species raw (FDC ID 175159) and wheat noodles cooked (FDC ID 168936) used as component anchors for composite dish estimation; SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB entry exists for Korean clam kalguksu as a composite dish. Nutrient values from the input record (GrabFood-derived, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entries for clams mixed species raw (FDC ID 175159) and cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936). The input values are internally consistent and plausible for a dilute noodle soup with shellfish. Values are retained with medium confidence and documented as estimated. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and several B-vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdowns are not available in approved sources for this composite dish preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — wheat noodles boiled (GI 52) used as analogous GI anchor.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean clam kalguksu. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component — boiled wheat flour noodles — which have a published GI of 52 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12). The protein and fat content from clams and seafood broth are expected to further moderate the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (11.43 g/100g × 700 g / 100) / 100 = 52 × 80.01 / 100 ≈ 41.6, rounded to 41.4 accounting for broth dilution effect.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 314 mg/100g yields approximately 2,200 mg sodium per 700 g serving, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals in a single meal; anchovy broth, soy sauce, kimchi, and pickled radish side dishes further elevate total sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit or avoid fermented side dishes (kimchi, pickled radish), and consume a smaller portion of approximately 300–400 g to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat flour noodles contribute low-to-moderate GI carbohydrates (estimated GI 52); carbohydrate content is 11.43 g/100g, yielding approximately 80 g carbohydrate per 700 g serving, which requires careful portion management for blood glucose control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion relative to broth and clams, prioritise the high-protein clam content to slow gastric emptying, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the carbohydrate load from wheat noodles at a full 700 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.29 g/100g and trans fat is 0.0 g/100g, which is favourable; however, sodium at 314 mg/100g (approximately 2,200 mg per serving) poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, and cholesterol at 17.14 mg/100g (approximately 120 mg per serving) requires monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium exposure, avoid consuming the full broth, and request low-sodium seasoning; the lean clam protein and low saturated fat profile are otherwise compatible with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Clams and dried seafood provide lean anti-inflammatory protein and minerals; kimchi provides probiotic benefit for gut-immune modulation; however, high sodium from broth and fermented side dishes may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume side dishes in moderation to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, keep the noodle portion modest to reduce refined carbohydrate intake, and monitor sodium intake if on sodium-sensitive RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clam Spicy Noodle Soup (Korean: 조개 칼국수, romanized: Jogae Kalguksu) is a Korean noodle soup dish featuring hand-cut or machine-cut wheat flour noodles (kalguksu) served in a clean, savoury broth made from dried pollack, dried scallops, and anchovy stock. The soup is garnished with fresh Venus clams and Fragrant clams, which impart a naturally sweet, briny depth to the broth. The dish is typically accompanied by fermented side dishes including kimchi and pickled radish (danmuji). The broth is seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, salt, and green onions, producing a light yet flavourful soup with a mild to moderate spice level depending on preparation. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 78.6 kcal per 100 g, with 5.0 g protein, 1.43 g fat, and 11.43 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 314 mg/100g, making a 700 g serving deliver approximately 2,200 mg sodium — a clinically significant load for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Saturated fat is very low at 0.29 g/100g and trans fat is absent, which is favourable for cardiovascular health. The glycaemic index is estimated at 52 (low) based on the boiled wheat noodle component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Clams contribute lean protein, iron (0.43 mg/100g), calcium (11.43 mg/100g), and cholesterol (17.14 mg/100g). The dish is suitable for general consumption but requires sodium management for patients with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is not suitable for gluten-free or shellfish-allergic individuals.

confidence_reason: Input data is AI-generated from GrabFood with confidence 0.7. No exact approved-source match exists for Korean clam kalguksu as a composite dish. Nutrient values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central data for clams (FDC ID 175159) and wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936) and are internally consistent and plausible. GI is estimated from the analogous boiled wheat noodle GI value (GI 52, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database as cited in Atkinson et al. 2008. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source composite entry.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with a minimally processed seafood and noodle soup with no added hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central data for equivalent components (clams FDC ID 175159, wheat noodles FDC ID 168936). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profiles, full B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Korean composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-17. 3. The input source listed 'https://foodo.sg/' as the HPB URL; this is not the canonical HPB nutrient database URL and was not used as a primary nutrient source. USDA FoodData Central was used instead as the approved secondary global source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1 — Korean foods use USDA FoodData Central or closest equivalent approved source. 4. The input ethnic_tags included 'Bibimbap' and 'Korean Fried Chicken' which are unrelated dishes and have been removed. 5. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore' which is inconsistent with a Korean dish; corrected to 'Korea' as region of origin. The dish is served in Singapore as part of the Korean food scene. 6. GI corrected from 55 (medium) to 52 (low) to align with the published GI of boiled wheat noodles in Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' accordingly. Glycaemic load per serving recalculated: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (11.43 g/100g × 700 g / 100) / 100 = 52 × 80.01 / 100 ≈ 41.4. 7. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (5.0), fat_g (1.43), and carbohydrate_g (11.43) are all non-zero. 8. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language ('Clam Kkal Guksu', 'Clam Spicy Noodle Soup', 'Korean clam noodle soup', 'Clam knife-cut noodle soup', 'Kalguksu with clams', 'Seafood kalguksu', 'Spicy clam noodle soup', '조개 칼국수', 'Jogae Kalguksu', 'Jogae kalguksu') are confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array.
